title: Retirement Calculator for Texas Residents: 2026 Guide
metaDescription: Free retirement calculator for Texas residents. Account for zero state income tax, tax-free Social Security, COL index 92, and TRS / ERS in your Texas retirement plan.

content.introduction:
Retirement planning in Texas isn't just about how much you save — it's about how much you keep. The Lone Star State has no state income tax, giving retirees a significant edge over high-tax states. With a cost of living index of 92 (100 = national average), your retirement dollar goes further in the Lone Star State.

A retirement calculator for texas residents needs to factor in these state-specific variables — not just generic 7% returns and 4% withdrawal rates. This guide covers exactly how Texas's tax structure, pension systems, Social Security treatment, and cost of living change your retirement math, with worked examples using real Texas numbers.

sections.heading: Texas's Retirement Tax Landscape
sections.content:
Texas has no state income tax, meaning every dollar of retirement income — Social Security, pensions, 401(k) withdrawals — stays fully intact at the state level.

Key tax factors for Texas retirees:

  • Income Tax: No state income tax — one of 9 US states with zero income tax
  • Social Security: No state income tax means Social Security, pensions, and all retirement income are tax-free at the state level.
  • Estate/Inheritance Tax: No state estate or inheritance tax.
  • Senior Property Tax Relief: $40,000 additional homestead exemption for 65+; school district taxes frozen at 65+; optional 65+ tax ceiling in cities/counties.

This zero-tax environment means a $1 million portfolio using the 4% rule generates $40,000/year with no state tax bite — compared to $0 in state taxes that a high-tax state would take.

sections.heading: How Much Do You Need to Retire in Texas?
sections.content:
The answer depends on your lifestyle, but here's the math using Texas-specific cost data:

  • Median household income: $73,035
  • Cost of living index: 92 (below national average)
  • Target retirement income: 80% of pre-retirement income = $53,754/year
  • Portfolio needed (4% rule): $1,343,850

The 4% rule means withdrawing 4% of your portfolio annually, adjusted for inflation. For Texas residents earning the median income, you'd need roughly $1,343,850 invested at retirement to maintain your current lifestyle — with no additional state tax drag on withdrawals.

Social Security replaces roughly 40% of pre-retirement income for average earners, potentially reducing your needed portfolio to around $806,310.

sections.heading: TRS / ERS: State Pension Benefits
sections.content:
Texas's public pension system — TRS / ERS — plays a significant role for public employees. Teacher Retirement System of Texas covers 1.9M members. Employees Retirement System covers state workers. Both are defined-benefit plans.

If you're a Texas public employee, your retirement calculator inputs change significantly:

  • Pension income: Typically replaces 50-70% of final average salary for career employees
  • Social Security interaction: Some Texas public employees don't pay into Social Security — your pension is the primary replacement
  • Supplemental savings: Even with a pension, most financial planners recommend building a 457(b) or supplemental retirement account for flexibility

For private-sector workers, your retirement income comes entirely from personal savings (401(k), IRA, taxable accounts) plus Social Security. The retirement calculator helps you model exactly how much to save each month.

sections.heading: Retirement Savings Timeline for Texas Workers
sections.content:
Using Texas's median household income of $73,035 and a 15% savings rate:

Start AgeMonthly SavingsPortfolio at 65Monthly Income (4% rule)
25$913$2,396,455$7,988
30$913$1,644,363$5,481
35$913$1,113,834$3,713
40$913$739,595$2,465
45$913$475,606$1,585

Starting at 25 vs 35 nearly doubles your retirement portfolio — that's the power of compound interest over an extra decade. Every year of delay costs approximately $85,642 in final portfolio value.

Explore different timelines with our compound interest calculator to see exactly how compounding works for your situation.

sections.heading: Texas Cost of Living Impact on Retirement
sections.content:
Texas's cost of living index of 92 means retirees benefit from costs that are 8% below the national average. Your retirement savings stretch further in the Lone Star State.

What this means for your retirement number:

  • National benchmark: $1 million portfolio → $40,000/year (4% rule)
  • Adjusted for Texas: $1 million portfolio → effectively $43,478/year in purchasing power
  • Texas-adjusted target: To match $40,000 national purchasing power, you need $920,000

Texas's costs are manageable for well-planned retirees.

sections.heading: Tax-Advantaged Accounts: Maximize Your Texas Savings
sections.content:
Regardless of where you live, maximize these accounts before taxable investing:

  • 401(k)/403(b): $24,000 limit in 2026 ($32,000 if 50+) — employer match is free money
  • Traditional IRA: $7,500 limit ($8,500 if 50+) — tax-deductible at the federal level
  • Roth IRA: $7,500 limit — grows tax-free forever. In a no-tax state like Texas, the Roth advantage is slightly less dramatic since withdrawals from traditional accounts also face zero state tax.
  • HSA: $4,350 individual / $8,750 family — triple tax advantage for healthcare costs in retirement

529 Plans: No state income tax, so no 529 deduction — but Texas 529 plan (Lonestar Plan) has low fees.

For Texas residents, traditional accounts are particularly attractive since you get the federal tax deduction now and pay zero state tax on withdrawals later.

sections.heading: Common Retirement Planning Mistakes in Texas
sections.content:
• Ignoring state tax changes: While Texas currently has no income tax, don't assume this will last 30 years. Diversify across Roth and traditional accounts.
  • Underestimating healthcare costs: The average 65-year-old couple needs $315,000+ for healthcare in retirement, and this figure applies nationally regardless of COL
  • Forgetting inflation: At 3% inflation, $40,000/year buying power shrinks to $24,000 in 15 years. Your calculator should model inflation-adjusted withdrawals.
  • Over-relying on Social Security: The average Social Security benefit is ~$1,900/month. For Texas residents, that covers only 42% of estimated expenses.
  • Not accounting for Texas-specific costs: Regional cost variations can surprise Texas retirees.

content.practicalExample:
Retirement Scenario: Texas Worker, Age 35

Profile: A 35-year-old Texas resident earning $73,035/year, saving 15% for retirement.

Inputs:

  • Monthly contribution: $913
  • Years to retirement: 30
  • Expected return: 7% (historical stock market average)
  • Current savings: $50,000

Results at Age 65:

  • Portfolio value: $1,113,834
  • Annual withdrawal (4% rule): $44,553
  • Monthly retirement income: $3,713
  • State tax on withdrawals: $0 (no state income tax)
  • Plus Social Security (~$1,900/month at full retirement age)

Bottom line: Combined retirement income of ~$5,613/month with zero state tax — sufficient to cover estimated expenses.

Model your own scenario with our retirement calculator.
content.conclusion:
Retirement planning for Texas residents requires more than plugging numbers into a generic calculator. The Lone Star State's zero income tax, Social Security exemption, cost of living index of 92, and pension system (TRS / ERS) all materially change how much you need and how long your savings will last.

Start with our retirement calculator to model your specific situation — input your actual income, savings rate, and expected retirement age to see where you stand. Then use the compound interest calculator to visualize how starting today versus next year impacts your final number.

faqs.question: Does Texas tax Social Security benefits?
faqs.answer: No state income tax means Social Security, pensions, and all retirement income are tax-free at the state level. This is a meaningful advantage for Texas retirees, as roughly 40% of retirees depend on Social Security for the majority of their income.

faqs.question: What is a good retirement savings target for Texas?
faqs.answer: Based on Texas's cost of living index of 92 and median income of $73,035, a household targeting 80% income replacement needs approximately $1,343,850 in retirement savings (using the 4% rule). Social Security reduces this by roughly 40%, bringing the savings target to about $806,310. Adjust based on your actual expenses, healthcare needs, and desired lifestyle.

faqs.question: Is Texas a good state to retire in?
faqs.answer: Texas is highly attractive for retirees due to zero state income tax on all retirement income, including pensions and Social Security. The cost of living index of 92 means your money goes further than the national average. Consider: $40,000 additional homestead exemption for 65+; school district taxes frozen at 65+; optional 65+ tax ceiling in cities/counties.

faqs.question: How does TRS / ERS work?
faqs.answer: Teacher Retirement System of Texas covers 1.9M members. Employees Retirement System covers state workers. Both are defined-benefit plans. Texas's pension system covers public employees including teachers, state workers, and often local government employees. Benefits are typically calculated as a formula: years of service multiplied by a percentage (often 1.5-2.5%) multiplied by final average salary. Contact TRS / ERS directly for your specific benefit estimate, and use our retirement calculator to model how pension income supplements your personal savings.
